candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: In the circuit shown in Fig. 3.21, the capacitors are initially charged to a voltage $V_{0}$. At $t=0$ the switch is closed. Derive an expression for the voltage at point $A$ at a later time $t$.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object string rather than a concrete file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: Suppose at time $t$ the voltage drops across the two capacitors are $V_{1}$, $V_{2}$ and the currents in the three branches are $i_{1}$, $i_{2}$, $i_{3}$ as shown in Fig. 3.21. By Kirchhoff's laws and the capacitor equation we have $$\left\{\begin{array}{l} i_{1} R+i_{2} R-V_{2}=0 \\ i_{1} R-V_{1}=0 \\ i_{1}-i_{2}+i_{3}=0 \end{array}\right.$$ $$\left\{\begin{array}{l} i_{2}=-C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} \\ i_{3}=C \frac{d V_{1}}{d t} \end{array}\right.$$ Equations (2) and (5) give $$ i_{3}=R C \frac{d i_{1}}{d t} $$ This and Eqs. (3) and (4) give $$ i_{1}+C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t}+R C \frac{d i_{1}}{d t}=0 $$ From Eqs. (1) and (4) one has $$ i_{1}=\frac{1}{R} V_{2}+C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} $$ Substituting it into (6), we obtain $$ \frac{d^{2} V_{2}}{d t^{2}}+\frac{3}{R C} \frac{d V_{2}}{d t}+\frac{1}{R^{2} C^{2}} V_{2}=0 $$ Solving this equation we have $$ V_{2}=A e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}+B e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}, $$ and hence $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1} & =i_{1} R=V_{2}+R C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} \\ & =-\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} A e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{6}}{2} t/RC}-\frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} B e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}. \end{aligned} $$ Using the initial condition that at $t=0$ $$ V_{1}(0)=V_{2}(0)= \pm V_{0} $$ we obtain $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\mathrm{A}}= & V_{2}= \pm \frac{5-3 \sqrt{5}}{10} V_{0} e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC} \pm \frac{5+3\sqrt{5}}{10} V_{0} e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{6}}{2} t/RC} \\ & \approx \pm\left(1.17 e^{-0.38 t/RC}-0.17 e^{-2.62 t/RC}\right) V_{0}. \end{aligned}$$

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: Suppose at time $t$ the voltage drops across the two capacitors are $V_{1}$, $V_{2}$ and the currents in the three branches are $i_{1}$, $i_{2}$, $i_{3}$ as shown in Fig. 3.21. By Kirchhoff's laws and the capacitor equation we have $$\left\{\begin{array}{l} i_{1} R+i_{2} R-V_{2}=0 \\ i_{1} R-V_{1}=0 \\ i_{1}-i_{2}+i_{3}=0 \end{array}\right.$$ $$\left\{\begin{array}{l} i_{2}=-C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} \\ i_{3}=C \frac{d V_{1}}{d t} \end{array}\right.$$ Equations (2) and (5) give $$ i_{3}=R C \frac{d i_{1}}{d t} $$ This and Eqs. (3) and (4) give $$ i_{1}+C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t}+R C \frac{d i_{1}}{d t}=0 $$ From Eqs. (1) and (4) one has $$ i_{1}=\frac{1}{R} V_{2}+C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} $$ Substituting it into (6), we obtain $$ \frac{d^{2} V_{2}}{d t^{2}}+\frac{3}{R C} \frac{d V_{2}}{d t}+\frac{1}{R^{2} C^{2}} V_{2}=0 $$ Solving this equation we have $$ V_{2}=A e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}+B e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}, $$ and hence $$ \begin{aligned} V_{1} & =i_{1} R=V_{2}+R C \frac{d V_{2}}{d t} \\ & =-\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} A e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{6}}{2} t/RC}-\frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} B e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC}. \end{aligned} $$ Using the initial condition that at $t=0$ $$ V_{1}(0)=V_{2}(0)= \pm V_{0} $$ we obtain $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\mathrm{A}}= & V_{2}= \pm \frac{5-3 \sqrt{5}}{10} V_{0} e^{-\frac{3+\sqrt{5}}{2} t/RC} \pm \frac{5+3\sqrt{5}}{10} V_{0} e^{-\frac{3-\sqrt{6}}{2} t/RC} \\ & \approx \pm\left(1.17 e^{-0.38 t/RC}-0.17 e^{-2.62 t/RC}\right) V_{0}. \end{aligned}$$
